sentences.text: Brussels
Well we traveled to about three days before we got to, to Berlin, Alexanderplatz.
And when we got to-- we didn't have anything to eat or anything, nothing to drink or eat.
They just left us there in the train.
And when we got-- now about two and half days, it took.
When we got there, then they-- we got off the train, and they put handcuffs on us.
We were handcuffed to go from-- you know, from the train to prison.
Got to the prison.
They put us all in a big room at this-- in this prison.
And we stayed there for hours and hours-- I think the whole day.
And they came and got Simone.
And they said, come with us because you're going to the washroom to get washed.
No, I saw her in the train.
I told you one of the Gestapo came, knocked on the door.
And he said, [SPEAKING GERMAN], what is your name?
And I told my name.
He said, well come with me.
So I went with him, and here was Simone.
He brought me to Simone.
She would-- Like this was an old, old wooden train with-- you had them here in America too, probably-- with a door that you opened up like that and wooden seat-- you know, big two wooden seat.
And she was there with other people that I didn't know that had worked probably with us, but we didn't know them.
We didn't know everybody.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
So she cried and I cried.
And I asked her about my family.
And she said that they were OK, that her sister had seen, I think, my sister or my mother.
I don't know who-- which one it was, but she'd seen my family and that they were all right.
They were OK.
What kind of shape was Simone in?
Very thin and sad and didn't look like Simone anymore.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You could see she had suffered terribly.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She must have been full of scars all over, but her face was really very thin and sad.
We didn't have much time.
He just left us a few minutes together.
I wanted to know about my family.
I asked her about her family.
And he was right next to us, and we couldn't talk long.
And she wanted to give me something but couldn't.
She couldn't give it to me.
She had candy or something.
She wanted to give it to me.
He dragged me away.
That was it.
Never saw Simone after that.
[PAUSES FOR 20 SECONDS] Matter of fact, I never have nightmares.
I had one last night.
I think seeing that film and talking, you know, with you, I had a nightmare.
I hope they didn't hear me upstairs.
Once in a while, I get them, and I scream.
Pardon me?
About the camp, when I was so sick.
I had typhus-- endemic typhus, black spots all over me.
And I was in a-- the block, of course.
And I had a prisoner like me that was sick.
I don't know if she had typhus, but she was very, very sick.
She was moaning all the time, moaning and moaning.
And all at once-- we had-- we slept four in a bunk bed, two at the head and two at the feet.
And all at once, she wasn't moaning anymore.
But I was so sick myself, I thought, well maybe she's asleep.
So I tried, you know, to see if she was asleep, but she was dead.
So she had been dead next to me, I don't know how long.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We saw so many dead people that we didn't-- it didn't-- it didn't affect us anymore.
Terrible to say that, but it didn't.
When we went to-- to what-- we call it the washroom to go and get washed up if we could, we-- sometimes we had no water at all.
We had no-- no clean underwear or anything.
So we would cut, we'd tear pieces of our panties to use it as a-- you know, to get washed with.
And one day, we were going to the washroom, and one of the girls said, oh, stop.
Look.
See.
And we were going-- passing the washroom.
There was a poor, young-- young girl, maybe 16 years old.
She had hung herself with a piece of her garment.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we had to walk on the bodies to get to-- they had like fountains.
They had round fountains in the washrooms with all-- faucets, you know, so we could get a, a lot of us around there.
And one day we were there at the washroom.
And to get there, we had to walk on the bodies.
They just threw the bodies in there.
Not wrapped up or anything, just there.
I'm not lying to you.
This is the honest truth.
You can ask anybody that was in camps.
First of all, everything I've told you, I'm not lying.
Pardon me?
Oh, yeah.
Well, no, it wasn't the same thing.
Alice's husband, my daughter's husband, he was arrested too.
He was in Mauthausen.
And Mauthausen was-- Ravensbruck was a hard labor camp for women, only women.
Mauthausen, it was mixed.
Then, towards the end, I was there three, three-- over three years in Ravensbruck.
Towards the end, they put men on one side and women here on the other.
About a week, one week, I think.
Nothing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In a cell with other people, not long.
People that didn't even speak English or French nor anything.
Foreigners, you know, all kinds of people that I don't-- they hadn't probably worked on the-- they weren't underground workers.
I don't know what they were.
We were all mixed up.
Simone was gone.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] When I came back from liberated, I saw her sister.
Of course, her father died of sorrow, Simone's father.
And Nellie-- her sister was named Nellie-- and she considered me like almost like a sister.
She told me that Simone had suffered so much.
She said it was a horrible way to go.
But she said it was a blessing that-- that they took her.
Pardon me?
To Simone?
To my family?
Oh, no.
I never knew during three and a half years.
I didn't know anything.
We went to Moabit Prison.
And there, when we got to Moabit, oh, I'll always remember.
We didn't go three or four or more at the time.
They took me alone, the Gestapo, put me in a car, private military car.
And they drove me all around Berlin.
And everywhere they drove me, I could see it had been all bombed up by the good USA.
And the opera building was a beautiful building that was all--
So I said, oh that-- I told him, I said, that's the opera.
I said, I used to-- to dance at the opera, too.
He said, did you dance here?
I said, no, I wouldn't want to dance here.
[LAUGHS] So anyway, I saw-- that's the way I saw Berlin.
Otherwise, I didn't see-- I didn't know Berlin.
I had never danced in Berlin.
So anyway, they took me from Alexanderplatz to Moabit.
And there they took me down, way, way down.
Moabit Prison was the biggest prison in all Germany and the deepest one.
I think there were six, five or six flights below the ground so people couldn't hear what was going on.
So they took me there.
And all at once, the Gestapo, in front of a desk there, and he said-- asked me my name.
And I told him.
And he had papers, and he was taking me for somebody else.
I said.
That's not me.
My name is so-and-so, you know.
So anyway, I had to stand there for a while.
And I could see some poor men-- I think they were Jewish men-- they were-- they were hanging but with their arms in back like that.
And they were looking at me and shaking their head and saying like, you're going to get through.
You're going to go through this, too.
They thought they were going to do that to me, what they did to them.
I couldn't look at them anymore.
They were-- blood coming out of their mouth.
It was horrible.
Horrible.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Poor men.
They were just Jewish men.
Of course, they had probably done something, you know, against them.
But they didn't care.
As long as you were Jewish, they punished you, or they killed you.
You know that.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] In-- in Ravensbruck, we had Jewish mothers.
They had their children with them.
And they would come and pull these little children away from the mothers and bring them in the [GERMAN], the camp for the young.
They never see their children again.
That was it.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]
Pardon?
In Moabit?
I think about a week, not more.
I think, you know.
No.
No.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was it.
The book was closed there.
I was sentenced to death, and that was it.
I can't.
They told me I was sentenced to death.
They told me.
They didn't tell me how they were going to do it.
They just said that, [SPEAKING GERMAN], all this was forbidden, that I was lost for the world.
That was it, that the world was not-- didn't belong to me anymore.
In German, I can't tell you that.
None.
I knew it.
They had told me already in-- in Brussels that I was sentenced to death.
Pardon me?
Yes.
Yeah, here I am.
Well, God was good to me, brought me home.
It's what I always asked.
I said, please-- I know when I had typhus so bad-- I was-- I really-- I was dying.
I was really dying there.
I felt it.
And I asked Him, I said, please, God, bring me home.
Let me die at home.
And I was delirious, and I got out of my bunker.
And we were sleeping four, you know-- I got out.
And there were windows like that, but no window pane, just windows.
And I walked towards the end of-- of the bunkers and I was calling mama.
Mama, come and get me.
Mama, come and get me.
And the girls, the prisoners, they were telling me, Mickey, keep quiet.
Mickey, keep quiet.
I could hear my name.
And they were saying, in French, [SPEAKING FRENCH] And then one of them grabbed me.
She said, they're going to come and beat you up and they're going to maybe kill you.
Keep quiet.
Don't say-- so they put me in bed.
And once I got in bed-- back in the bunker with them, that I calmed down, and my-- my delirium sort of passed, you know.
I wanted my mother and-- I wanted my mother.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] [SIGHS] Poor mother.
She waited for me.

Remember when I-- well, you're not to the liberation, but when I was liberated, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she was jumping with joy, so much that at the train station in Paris-- because I was liberated in Belgium.
They brought me back to Belgium.
And John and his nephew came to wait for me at the air-- at the airport.
And we were several of us.
I wasn't alone.
And when we got there, there were hostess ladies, hostess to give us flowers, you know.
And we weren't-- we weren't dressed very nice because they'd taken our uniform off.
But in Sweden, they gave us other clothes, but not things that were very fancy.
Anyway, when I got there-- and poor-- they were both John.
His nephew and him were both John.
And they looked at me, and they said, oh, poor Mickey.
[LAUGHS] We have to go and get you some clothes.
And they want to make like a joke of it, you know.
But anyway, when I got there, then they had re-- reserved a hotel, a whole suite.
And John had put downstairs, 'forbidden to have visits.' I was too weak.
The doctor didn't want any visitors, nothing, and not-- not to talk to me too much, to leave me alone.
So anyway, I had a very nice dinner that night.
I'll always remember, nice dinner with the two Johns.
And then they left very early, and I-- I rested.
And then he said, well, tomorrow morning, we'll come and get you and we're going to take you to go and get all the clothes you want.
Everything you want, you get it.
So I did.
I went and got all dressed up in shoes and everything.
Because then John said, now I've gotten it-- I'm in touch with your family.
And they're expecting you, and now you can't stay here with us.
You have to get to Paris.
So they had made all the arrangements.
And when I got to Paris, my brother-in-law from the Guaranty Trust was there.
My sister Yvonne was there.
My daughter, of course, was there.
My mother was there.
Peter, my nephew that lives in Paris still, he was there.
He was a little boy then.
And my sister May was there.
And they had put-- I was in a special train, but there were trains coming in with-- with prisoners, you know, liberated prisoners.
But he didn't want me to go in that train.
He wanted me to take a Pullman and be in a, you know, regular train.
So I was in that train.
And they'd put gates so that people couldn't go and jump, you know-- catch the ex-prisoners and kiss them and all that there.
Not to make, you know, to get too many people together.
My mother saw that-- the barricade.
She jumped over it.
She jumped over it and she said, I have to go and get my daughter.
Let me get my daughter.
So the police officer was so amazed to see my mother-- she must have been about 70, over 70 years old anyway-- to jump like that that he let her go.
He said, please-- he said, mama, you go and get your daughter.
Go get your daughter.
And then I think that the whole night long, I spoke, the whole night long.
They asked me all kinds of things, of course.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was my liberation.
But we didn't have the liberation part with Count Bernadotte.
You didn't ask me that.
After Moabit, then we went to Alex-- to Ravensbruck.
From Moabit, we went to Ravensbruck.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Right?
Did you go to Ravensbruck, or did you go to Mauthausen?
Yeah, only for-- I told you, only for a few weeks.
I don't count that even.
I don't count it because it wasn't really my camp.
My camp was Ravensbruck.
Well let's talk a little bit about what you saw when you were at Mauthausen.
I saw especially the men.
More men were working than women.
And the men were working-- it-- Mauthausen is built on all kinds-- like-- like hills-- and like how do you call where they dig stones?
Yeah, like-- how do you say that?
Yeah.
I can't say that in English anyway.
Some words, I can't say.
But anyway, that's what it is.
Mauthausen is that.
They make all kinds of-- they dig up stones and rocks and all that.
That's what the men did in Mauthausen.
We did very-- they didn't make us hardly work there.
We didn't do much.
Oh, yeah.
Yeah.
Well, they weren't-- they weren't so strict in Mauthausen with us because they knew we weren't going to stay there.
Yeah, the men were working hard, but we had-- we did it, too, in Ravensbruck.
We had to do it-- not-- not rocks, but knocked down trees.
We had to build the whole buildings, the women did.
Chop, chop rocks, I can't tell you, with big-- you know, what do you call them?
I don't know in English the words.
I'm sorry.
In French, we call it [SPEAKING FRENCH].
I can't say it in English, you know.
But you-- it's like a-- like a hammer, but it's pointy.
And they'd knock on that, and knock on that, and knock on that.
And if they stopped, the Gestapo was in back, come on.
Hurry up.
Work, you know.
Or they hit them.
They would hit them.
They'd beat them up if they didn't go enough, didn't do enough.
I don't know.
They'd go back to their barracks, I suppose, you know.
Yeah, I didn't stay there too long.
As I say, we weren't there to stay.
We were like-- Auschwitz, some people stayed.
We went-- we went through Auschwitz, but we didn't stay either.
It was like a transfer, you know?
Maybe a day.
In a train, we never got off.
We went to Alexan-- Alexanderplatz.
It was on the way to Alexander.
That's what I said, took us about three days to get there.
Ravensbruck.
We got there at night, and they put us in what they call the washroom.
And it was cold.
It was raining.
It was terrible.
Great big-- I want to say great big gates.
And once they closed that gate on you, you knew that was it.
So we got to-- there at night, and they put us in that washroom.
And we stayed there till the morning, in that washroom, sitting where you could sit, you know.
And then they-- they told us that we'd go to such-and-such a block.
And our first block was number two, the number two.
And there, we had a horrible head of the block.
She was a-- we called her golden teeth.
She all gold teeth and she had been a guardian in prison for, for, for murderers.
She was horrible.
But she got a liking to me.
I don't know why.
[LAUGHS] She was a little bit nicer with me because she liked me.
I don't know why.
But we stayed there-- that were-- only the ones that were sentenced to death went to that block.
Nobody else.
Pardon me?
At night.
I don't know what date.
I can't tell you.
I can-- I mean, I can't remember all that.
You have, you have have all the dates.
Everything's written there, but I can't remember.
Pardon?
Yes.
Yes, she was.
She was wicked.
She was a little skinny woman with white hair, all gold teeth.
We called her-- I'm going to be vulgar because I think that's what you'd say in English-- golden mug, we called her like that.
She liked me.
I don't know why.
No.
No, but she didn't beat me up.
[LAUGHS] I didn't get any favors, though.
I had to go to work like everybody else.
My first job, wheelbarrow work on hills like that.
Putting dirt in the wheelbarrows, going up a hill, and dumping it.
Go down, get another one, and up, for nothing, nothing.
Just to wear you out, you know.
That was my first job.
Then after that, well, we had to knock down trees.
Sometimes we did that.
Every morning, we went off with-- with a shovel and a thing on our, on our shoulder after being counted.
They counted us in the camp night and noon and morning.
You had to stand.
They call that the [SPEAKING GERMAN] and you had to stay still, not talk, nothing, nothing, nothing.
We did anyway.
Pardon?
Yes, yeah.
No, not with us.
In the camp, yes, but not in our block, no.
They were Polish women all sentenced to death-- lawyers, doctors, that all worked for the underground.
There were French, Belgium and Polish in my block.
No.
No, I didn't know them, no.
Yes, some Polish girls that came and lived in Brussels, and they lived in my-- the same building I did.
[INAUDIBLE] and, and [INAUDIBLE].
[INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE].
But they-- one of them went back home, went back to Poland.
And the other one, I think, got married, but I-- I lost trace of her, because I didn't stay there.
I went back to Paris.
Oh, yeah, we tried to help each other.
Well, sometimes one had a-- would be lucky and get a pair of, of what?-- socks or stockings or whatever.
If she could and one didn't have it, she'd give it to the other.
That's all we could do.
We never had food.
We couldn't give, you know, food.
We couldn't do that.
Sometimes we had-- we'd, we'd use our-- we'd get one little tiny piece of soap that was made with human, human grease-- how would I say-- human fat?
And we'd get one piece a week.
And my hair-- they'd shaved all my hair off.
I had long hair, and they shaved it like that and they showed me.
They said, oh, sure, now.
It was pretty that way.
So anyway, I said, my friends, I want my hair to grow.
So I used my-- I'd tell them-- use that soap like shampoo.
So they'd massage my-- my head with the soap.
[LAUGHS] Yeah, we-- we had some laughters too.
We didn't always cry.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And at night, we'd be punished because when we went to bed, we'd all-- I think it was 8:00, no more noise or anything.
You couldn't talk, no noise.
But then we would sing.
We'd sing one religious song or something to put us to sleep.
And they'd come in with the dogs and they'd-- oh, they were so cruel with us.
They'd make us keep quiet.
We didn't even have that-- the liberty to sing, say goodnight, or even say a prayer, you know?
Now, you spoke-- you spoke a number of languages.
Yes.
When I first got there?
Not in-- yeah, in Ravensbruck.
No, I-- first jobs I had were all hard labor, not good jobs.
I only got the good job at Siemens later on.
I was sick after that.
I had the hard labor and then I fell sick with typhus, then I had endemic typhus.
And I had blood poison, my two legs, I told you.
I had worms coming out of my-- my legs, out of the holes in my legs.
Hm?
OK.
